title: Born on Grandmother's Slot of Land
summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin on the campus of Albany State University in March 2013, Mary Jones recalls being born in Albany, Georgia, on July 24, 1933, and being reared by her grandmother Bertha McIntosh on a small plot of land the family had bought, a place where churchgoing and weekend work went together.

title: Picking Cotton and Pulling Corn
summary: Jones describes leaving school each fall to work the white man's farm gathering peanuts, picking cotton, digging potatoes, pulling corn, and gathering pecans, missing the first two months of school until the harvest was in around October or November, and starting that work at about eight years old under the taskmaster Miss Alice Porter.

title: Seventy-Five Cents for School Clothes
summary: She recalls hoeing the grass around bird seed planted on a small farm owned by Mr. Kina, who later became mayor, earning about seventy-five cents that the children saved to buy school clothes, working from before sunup to around five, fed on the job, and walking together home through the neighborhood.

title: School in the Baptist Church
summary: Jones recounts attending school inside one of the oldest Baptist churches in Southwest Georgia, connected to W.E.B. Du Bois, the grades divided by corners of the one building up through seventh grade at a school named Shepherd after the man who gave the congregation the ground, with teachers like Miss Willie Mae Jackson and Miss Celeste Scott who kept the children in line.

title: Married the Tuesday After the Friday Game
summary: She describes coming into town to attend Monroe Comprehensive High School, staying with her aunt Ella Holloway and three cousins in the Black community, playing basketball and running track, and falling in love with Jimmy Lee Jones, whom she married in 1951 instead of finishing her senior year, days before he left for the Army.

title: The Principal Called Them Monkeys
summary: Jones tells of following news of integration through the radio and the Pittsburgh Courier and A.C. Searles's Southwest Georgian, wanting better for her children, and signing her oldest daughter into the newly integrated high school, where Principal McNair called the Black students little monkeys and they faced fights nearly every day.

title: She Hung In There
summary: She recalls her daughter being spit at and called names in the cafeteria but hanging in there because friends had registered alongside her, and describes how black and white teachers were transferred between schools, though only one or two Black teachers went to the formerly white schools and her daughter found she could no longer play basketball there.

title: A House Mama Raising Somebody
summary: Jones describes her husband serving two years and later being recalled to active duty as a reservist during the movement, while she stayed home as a house mama running the children to school and meetings, teaching them that they were somebody regardless of skin color, that God loves everyone, and to go to school to make something of themselves.

part: Voter Registration and the Criterion Club
title: After Rosa Parks, the Criterion Club
summary: Having heard of the Montgomery incident and Rosa Parks, Jones recounts the Criterion Club coming together and her work in voter registration, including a small school set up in an office to teach people to read because applicants had to read the Georgia Constitution to register.

part: Voter Registration and the Criterion Club
title: Registered Is Not Voted
summary: She describes canvassing the projects where she lived, reaching people too scared to go register because of the reading requirement, building their trust so they would come to her, and the disappointment of a woman she carried to register who never actually voted, teaching Jones that registering alone counts for nothing.

title: Seven Sent to See Pritchett
summary: Jones tells of serving on the movement's police committee, one of seven members chosen to talk with Chief Laurie Pritchett, who ignored them the first several visits while a deputy left them sitting for hours, until McCree Harris, who worked at Monroe High and was known to him, pressed him and he claimed no Black applicants had come forward.

title: Walking a Beat With No Patrol Car
summary: She recalls McCree Harris taking applications door to door so that about five Black officers were hired, among them Etheridge Smith, David Hyde, and Robert Benning Jr., though they were given no patrol cars and made to walk a beat, and explains the committee acted because Black men were being killed nearly every week, often by white police.

title: Choosing Dr. Anderson to Lead
summary: Jones names the leaders of the Criterion Club who, already active in registration, helped organize the Albany Movement, including Dr. William G. Anderson, C.B. and Slater King, Thomas Chapman, and Marion S. Page, and recalls choosing Dr. Anderson as president of the movement and Mr. Page as secretary.

title: Welcoming the Freedom Riders
summary: She describes the Freedom Riders arriving by train and being arrested as the community went down to welcome them, the mass meetings held most nights, and the church burnings that frightened some ministers off, after which Dr. King and his organizations helped rebuild the burned churches more soundly than before.

title: The Children Marched First
summary: Jones recalls anger at James Gray's paper claiming the grown people pushed the children out front because they were too scared to march, the adults organizing right there at a mass meeting, and roughly seven hundred of them marching from the corner of Washington and Broad Avenue past the old jail.

title: Seven Hundred in a Tiny Jail
summary: She tells of marching even after being warned to stop and go home, being arrested and held from Monday to Thursday, taken to Baker County jail, the Billy clubs, standing in pouring rain for hours before being booked, and packed with about twenty-five people into a cell built for eight or ten with one bed.

title: Singing Louder Than the Dogs
summary: Jones describes the jailers threatening to loose dogs on the prisoners and the marchers singing and praying louder in response, the hard black-eyed peas and grits with bread thrown on top they were fed, and her resolve that it was rough but worth it, trying to march again only to find they would no longer arrest her.

title: A Black Christmas Downtown
summary: She recounts the white friends who quietly helped, the rule that anyone who sided with Black people got treated as they were, the leaders' rebuffed attempts to talk with Pritchett and the city manager, and the boycott in which people cut ties with downtown, got their layaway goods out before Christmas, and shopped instead in Macon.

title: My Father Bonded Me Out
summary: Jones describes the relief of seeing her children after her father bonded her out, her suspicion that officials profited from the bail money, and how she stayed involved once she could no longer be arrested by hosting daily meetings at her home in the Washington projects and serving as a voting captain who told her neighbors who to vote for.

title: Good Jobs and Cash Registers
summary: Looking back, Jones counts the movement a success rather than a failure, pointing to integrated schools, parents raising their children up right, and good jobs opening so that Black people could run cash registers instead of only cleaning, and names the legacy she wants remembered: much prayer, sticking together, and leaving the old Uncle Tom ways behind.

title: The Teachers Who Could Not March
summary: She explains that the superintendent forbade teachers to march on pain of losing their jobs, so instead they supported the marchers by buying food and paying light bills and rent, and tells of an older woman who, with no money tied up in stores to boycott, went to jail several times and had her rent paid and food waiting when she got out.

title: Where My Education Came From
topic: Head Start and the War on Poverty
summary: Jones closes by crediting the movement and the War on Poverty under President Johnson and Sargent Shriver, through which she went into early childhood education with Head Start, for her own schooling, and explains why she stands up whenever there is talk of cutting Head Start funds.
